STEM EDUCATION
229. DR. AMAR SINGH:
Will the Minister of EDUCATION be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government is aware that storytelling can serve as a powerful tool to
demystify complex STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics)
concepts, foster critical thinking and ignite a passion for discovery;
(b) if so, the details thereof; and
(c) the initiatives proposed to be taken by the Government to integrate storytelling into
STEM education keeping in mind that narrative techniques help children better
understand and retain scientific information, making abstract ideas tangible and
relatable?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF SKILL DEVELOPMENT AND
ENTREPRENEURSHIP; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF
EDUCATION (SHRI JAYANT CHAUDHARY):
(a) to (c) The Government recognizes the effectiveness of storytelling in making children
understanding the complex concepts and fostering critical thinking as a part of the STEM
(Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics) Education. The National
Education Policy (NEP) 2020 provides that assessments of educational approaches in
undergraduate education that integrate the humanities and arts with Science,
Technology, Engineering and Mathematics (STEM) have consistently showed positive
learning outcomes, including increased creativity and innovation, critical thinking and
higher-order thinking capacities, problem-solving abilities, teamwork, communication
skills, more in-depth learning and mastery of curricula across fields, increases in social
03.02.2025 1176
and moral awareness, etc., besides general engagement and enjoyment of learning.
Research is also improved and enhanced through a holistic and multidisciplinary
education approach.
The National Curriculum Framework for School Education (NCF-SE) 2023 states
that stories are a particularly good medium for learning about social relationships, ethical
choices, understanding and experiencing emotions, and becoming aware of life skills.
While listening to stories, children learn new words, thus expanding their vocabulary,
and learn sentence structure and problem-solving skills. Children with very short
attention spans concentrate for a longer time when engrossed in a story. Through
culturally contextual stories, we can acquaint children with their culture and social norms
and create awareness about their surroundings. NCERT uses storytelling pedagogy in
almost every subject area including science and mathematics for creating interesting
narratives for children. The vision to create a joyful and expressive learning environment,
contributing to the overall development of students and society has led to introduction of
Traditional Story telling in the 10th National Kala Utsava, a flagship programme of the
Department of School Education and Literacy (DSEandL), Ministry of Education (MoE),
in 2024. In textbooks developed as a follow-up of the NCF-SE 2023, storytelling
pedagogy has been integrated. The Science textbook for Grade 6, namely, ‘Curiosity’,
and its Hindi version ‘Jigyasa’, presents concept using storytelling mode.
Web link of textbooks: Curiosity: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?fecu1=0-12
Jigyasa: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?fhcu1=0-12
03.02.2025 1177
UNCONTROLLED INFLATION
230. DR. KIRSAN NAMDEO:
DR. KALANIDHI VEERASWAMY:
SHRI BALYA MAMA SURESH GOPINATH MHATRE:
SHRI DINESH CHANDRA YADAV:
Will the Minister of FINANCE be pleased to state:
(a) whether common people are facing a lot of difficulties due to uncontrolled inflation
and the cost of meals for the common people has increased due to 25-50 per cent rise
in prices of food items, groceries and household items;
(b) if so, the details thereof;
(c) the steps taken/being taken by the Government to control inflation and prices of
essential commodities such as food items, fuel, etc.;
(d) whether low income, unemployment, high cost of petroleum products and low
productivity of crops due to climate change are the main reasons for high inflation and
price rises in the country;
(e) if so, the details thereof;
(f) whether the Government is aware of the huge impact of rising commodity prices on
the country, leading to stress among people, business facing losses and some even
shutting down due to increased costs; and
(g) whether the Goods and Services Tax (GST) has imparted the rising prices of goods
and services in the country?
03.02.2025 1178
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF FINANCE (SHRI PANKAJ
CHAUDHARY):
(a) and (b) Headline retail price inflation, which is based on the prices of food, fuel and
other items of household consumption, moderated to 4.9 per cent in 2024-25 (April-
December), compared to 5.4 per cent in 2023-24, making essential items more
affordable to the common man
(c) The government is regularly taking measures to control prices of essential
commodities. These measures include building buffers of key food items, periodically
releasing these items into the open market, adopting trade policies to enhance domestic
food availability, preventing hoarding by setting and revising stock limits, distributing
select food items at subsidised prices through designated retail outlets and periodic
reduction in the retail selling prices of domestic LPG.
(d) and (e) The per capita net national income of the country at constant prices is
estimated to grow at an average rate of 6.3 per cent during 2023-24 and 2024-25. As
per the Periodic Labour Force Survey, the unemployment rate in usual status has
declined from 4.1 per cent in 2021-22 to 3.2 per cent in 2023-24. Retail inflation in
petroleum products (LPG, diesel and petrol) was negative during April-December 2024.
However, food inflation is affected by weather-affected production losses and supply
chain disruptions.
(f) Retail inflation moderated from 2023-24 to 2024-25 (April-December). The wholesale
price inflation that broadly tracks the cost of production remained low at 2.2 per cent
during 2024-25 (April-December). The number of unincorporated non-agricultural
enterprises increased from 6.5 crore in 2022-23 to 7.34 crore in 2023-24 as per the
03.02.2025 1179
Annual Survey of Unincorporated Sector Enterprises. Similarly, the number of factories
in the country also increased from 2021-22 to 2022-23, as per the Annual Survey of
Industries.
(g) The GST rates on a number of items have been suitably changed from time to time
by the GST Council to provide relief to consumers.

(i) Need to formulate a comprehensive policy for rehabilitation of people
whose land was acquired for industrial purposes in Jharkhand

(cid:255)भािवत प(cid:229)रवार(cid:332) के पनु वा(cid:170)स और उनके अिधकार(cid:332) क(cid:236) र(cid:177)ा के िवषय पर कुछ श(cid:202)द रखना चाहता ह(cid:243) ँ ।

लेिकन इस िवकास क(cid:236) क(cid:236)मत पर जो हमारे िकसान और आिदवासी भाई-बहन अपनी ज़मीन खो चकु े
ह(cid:167), उनके जीवन म(cid:164) अ(cid:194)यिधक क(cid:310) और असमंजस पैदा हो रहा है । हम सभी जानते ह(cid:167) िक जब-जब भूिम

(ii) Need to increase the distance between two toll plazas on national
highways to 100 kilometres
(cid:174)ी िदलीप शइक(cid:236)या (दारगं -उदालगुड़ी) : देश के नेशनल हाईवे पर इस व(cid:265) 983 टोल (cid:200)लाजा चल
रहे ह(cid:167) । वत(cid:170)मान म(cid:164) इन टोल (cid:200)लाज(cid:332) पर वाहन चालक(cid:332) को भारी भरकम टोल चुकाना पड़ रहा है, िजससे
देश के आम नाग(cid:229)रक(cid:332) पर आिथ(cid:170)क बोझ बढ़ रहा है । टोल दर(cid:332) म(cid:164) वािष(cid:170)क (cid:214)तर पर संशोधन हो रहा है,
िजससे आव(cid:212)यक व(cid:214)तुओ ंक(cid:236) प(cid:229)रवहन लागत भी बढ़ रही है और आम नाग(cid:229)रक(cid:332) पर अित(cid:229)र(cid:265) बोझ पड़
रहा है । वत(cid:170)मान म(cid:164) देश के कुल रा(cid:213)(cid:367)ीय राजमाग(cid:170) क(cid:236) लंबाई लगभग 1,46,000 िकलोमीटर ह,ै जो दूसरा
सबसे बड़ा वैि(cid:309)क सड़क नेटवक(cid:170) है । वष(cid:170) 2018-19 म(cid:164) टोल सं(cid:250)ह करीब 25 हजार करोड़ (cid:321)पये था, जो
वष(cid:170) 2022-23 म(cid:164) बढ़कर 54 हजार करोड़ (cid:321)पये हो गया था । क(cid:164) (cid:254) सरकार के िदशा-िनद(cid:165)श(cid:332) के अनसु ार
2 टोल (cid:200)लाजा के म(cid:197)य कम से कम 60 िकमी. क(cid:236) दूरी होनी चािहए, लेिकन असम रा(cid:186)य म(cid:164) गवु ाहाटी

रा(cid:213)(cid:367)ीय राजमाग(cid:170) पर के वल 50 िकमी. के म(cid:197)य 2 टोल (cid:200)लाजा चल रहे है । क(cid:164) (cid:254) सरकार से अनुरोध है िक
देश के नाग(cid:229)रक(cid:332) पर बढ़ते टोल (cid:200)लाजा और टोल टै(cid:179)स के भार को कम करने और 2 टोल (cid:200)लाज(cid:332) के

(vi) Need to bring a comprehensive policy for proper distribution of human
organs obtained through donations
SHRI PARSHOTTAMBHAI RUPALA (RAJKOT): In the rural areas of our country,
thousands of poor people are blind and disabled. These individuals are not only
physically suffering but are also in an extremely vulnerable position socially and
economically. If these differently-abled individuals could receive donations of
human organs and eyes, it could bring a new hope and light into their lives.
However, unfortunately, thousands of poor blind and disabled individuals in rural
03.02.2025 1220
areas are not able to receive donations of human organs and eyes. The main
reason for this is that they lack information about this process. Due to a lack of
awareness, they miss out on this important opportunity. Additionally, the facilities
for organ and eye transplants in Government hospitals are also very limited.
Because of lack of facilities in Government Hospitals, those who could receive help
through donations also, remain unable to do transplantations. So far, no concrete
policy has been implemented for the distribution of human organs obtained through
organ donation, which is why the poor are not able to benefit from these organs.
The Central Government should work with State Governments to create a clear and
transparent policy in this direction.

(viii) Need to recognize the aborigines of Santhal Parganas as
Scheduled Tribes
DR. NISHIKANT DUBEY (GODDA): I want to place a piece of great historical record –
a book titled: ”The Little World of an Indian District Officer”, written by R. Carstairs and
03.02.2025 1221
published by Macmillan & Co., London in 1912. In this book there is a detailed, historical
record of the fact that the Santhal Parganas was created and named in 1855, and thus
was the youngest of the Bengal districts. The writer provides a wonderful account and
description of the Ghatwals (guardians of the passes) and the Khetowrie (Khetauri) and
how at the time of the Permanent settlement in 1790, every part of the territory was
occupied. This details that at the time of the Permanent Settlement, there was NOT a
single Sonthal in the whole of this area. “Bhunyas, Khetowries, Hindoos, Mahomedans,
Highlanders – yes, but Sonthals, no”. It is a fact that when these findings were recorded
and when the book in question was published, the dispensation of Scheduled Castes
and Tribes did not exist in the context of what it means administratively today. Thus the
aborigines of the region are the ones who are deprived of their rightful status and claim
to be recognized as Scheduled Tribes.
(ix) Need to establish a regulatory authority for private coaching institutes
DR. K. SUDHAKAR (CHIKKBALLAPUR): In recent decades, coaching centers have
proliferated across various regions, including metropolitan areas as well as tier 2 and
tier 3 cities. The unchecked expansion of these institutions, which aim to assist students
in successfully navigating national-level entrance examinations such as NEET and the
Joint Entrance Examination (JEE), as well as state-level engineering entrance tests, has
raised significant concerns. Under the guise of providing coaching services, many of
these institutions exploit students and their families financially. Despite the Union
Government's introduction of guidelines requiring the establishment of websites for the
registration of coaching centers and the prohibition of misleading advertisements, these
institutions often exploit legal loopholes, leading families into precarious debt situations.
03.02.2025 1222
It is imperative for the Government of India to establish a regulatory authority for private
coaching institutes. There must be stringent regulations concerning coaching fees,
unethical practices, standards of coaching, exorbitant pricing, and the high-stress
environment often associated with these institutions. Furthermore, regulation should
also encompass competitive coaching establishments. I urge the Government of India
to take proactive measures in establishing entrance coaching institutes specifically
designed for the economically disadvantaged and most vulnerable segments of society.
(x) Need for comprehensive measures to curb cyber crimes in the country
SHRI P. P. CHAUDHARY (PALI): I draw the attention of the Hon'ble Minister of
Home Affairs to the alarming rise in digital scams targeting our senior citizens.
Recent cases reveal a disturbing pattern where fraudsters exploit the digital
vulnerability of the elderly. A retired engineer in Delhi lost ₹10 crore through an
investment scam, a 72-year-old in Chennai was duped of ₹4.72 crore through fake
banking alerts, and an 82-year-old in Hyderabad lost ₹1.38 crore to impersonators
claiming to be Government officials. These scammers often use sophisticated
tactics, including deep-fake technology and official-looking documents, making it
increasingly difficult for seniors to distinguish legitimate communications from
fraudulent ones. The lack of accessible digital literacy resources, combined with
isolation and limited access to immediate verification mechanisms, makes our
elderly particularly vulnerable. Current cybercrime reporting systems, while robust,
often prove challenging for seniors to navigate effectively. I urge the Ministry to
establish Physical Aadhaar Assistance Centers with dedicated senior citizen help
desks, implement real-time transaction monitoring systems, and create a simplified
03.02.2025 1223
emergency response mechanism for immediate fund freezing. Additionally, we
need mandatory digital literacy programs at bank branches and regular security
audits of Aadhaar-linked services.

(xiii) Need for establishment of an Ayurvedic Medical College in Kandhamal
district of Odisha
SHRI SUKANTA KUMAR PANIGRAHI (KANDHAMAL): I would like to bring
Government’s attention to a pressing matter regarding the establishment of a new
Ayurvedic Medical college in the tribal district of Kandhamal, Odisha. This region is
renowned for its rich biodiversity and abundance of medicinal herbs, making it an
ideal location for an institution dedicated to the study and research of Ayurveda.
The objectives of this proposed college would be to provide education and training
in Ayurvedic Medical, and facilitate research in the field of Ayurveda, with a focus
on the medicinal herbs found in the Kandhamal region, to establish a conservatory
for the medicinal herbs found in the region and promote sustainable harvesting
practices to ensure their availability for future generations and to provide healthcare
services to the local residents, utilizing the principles of Ayurveda and promoting
holistic health and wellness. I urge the Minister of AYUSH to consider this proposal
and provide necessary support for the establishment of the Ayurvedic Medical
college in Aspirational Kandhamal district. This initiative would not only promote the
development of Ayurveda but also contribute to the socio-economic development
of the region.
(xiv) Need for upgradation and modernization of Changanassery Market
in Kerala
SHRI KODIKUNNIL SURESH (MAVELIKKARA): I would like to draw the kind
attention of the House to the pressing need for the upgradation and modernization
03.02.2025 1225
of the Changanassery Market, which serves as a vital hub for the trade of fish and
vegetables in the region. The current state of the market is inadequate to meet the
needs of both vendors and consumers. Poor infrastructure, lack of hygienic
conditions, and insufficient storage facilities are significant concerns. The absence
of modern amenities such as proper waste management systems, cold storage
units for perishables, and well-ventilated trading spaces is affecting the livelihoods
of vendors and the quality of produce available to the public. I urge the Government
to allocate necessary funds and initiate a comprehensive development plan for the
Changanassery Market. This plan should include the construction of modern stalls,
installation of cold storage facilities, provision for proper drainage and waste
disposal systems, and the creation of a clean and organized trading environment.
Such measures will not only improve the market's efficiency but also enhance
public health and ensure fair prices for both consumers and traders. I request the
concerned Ministry to prioritize this matter and take immediate action to ensure the
upgradation of the Changanassery Market.

(xvii) Need to address the plight of farmers in the country
SHRI AMRINDER SINGH RAJA WARRING (LUDHIANA): I am deeply concerned
about the plight of farmers in various parts of our Country. For years now, the brave
farmers of Punjab, Haryana, and UP have been protesting in harsh conditions.
They demand what is rightfully theirs—fair prices for their crops, dignity in their
work, and respect for their labour. I stand here in solidarity with farmer leaders who
have been sitting on a fast unto death & several farmers who have made the
03.02.2025 1227
ultimate sacrifice as a testament to the dire urgency of this cause. These demands
are so pressing, because agriculture in India is failing. Once a proud sector, it has
become a loss-making endeavour. Rising input costs, falling market prices, and the
lack of Government support have left farmers struggling for survival. I urge Central
Government to act now and when they sit with farmer leaders on 14th February
they must do so with a desire and honesty to fulfil their just demands and provide
them relief.
(xviii) Need to bring legislation for protection of humans from wild animal
menace in the country
SHRI ANTO ANTONY (PATHANAMTHITTA): I rise to bring to the attention of the
Government to the growing attacks by wild animals on humans, which pose a
serious threat to lives and livelihoods, particularly for people living near forest
areas. Recent incidents occurred on last week in Mananthavady, Wayanad District
where a woman named Radha, who was plucking coffee at the Priyadarshini Estate
was attacked and killed by a tiger and a man named Mani (40) killed by wild
elephant in Malappuram District. The number of wild animal attacks are increasing
every day in Kerala. This is not an isolated case. Across the country, there has
been an increase in such incidents, resulting in loss of life and injuries. These
attacks are creating fear among people living near forests, disrupting their daily
lives and livelihoods. The farmers are fully dependent on their cultivation and if it is
destroyed, they do not have any other choice to survive. The Government needs
to revise the estimation methods for crop loss due to wild animals to ensure fair
compensation for affected farmers. I urge the Government to introduce a
03.02.2025 1228
comprehensive law to protect humans from wildlife attacks and safeguard
livelihoods.

03.02.2025 1230
(xxi) Need to expedite introduction and passage of Prevention of Cruelty to
Animals (Amendment) Bill, 2022
SHRIMATI JUNE MALIAH (MEDINIPUR): I urgently request the expedited
introduction and passage of the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (Amendment) Bill,
2022. Released for public consultation on 21.11.2022, this Bill is crucial for
enhancing animal protection and promoting societal welfare.
The Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960 has not been updated for
over sixty years, rendering it ineffective in the face of rising incidents of animal
cruelty. Current penalties, ranging from 10 to 100, are grossly inadequate and do
not serve as a deterrent. Furthermore, many offences remain non-cognizable and
bailable, allowing offenders to evade accountability. This fosters a troubling mindset
that disregards legal boundaries, posing risks to societal wellbeing.
The proposed Bill includes vital reforms such as increased penalties, the
introduction of additional cognizable offences and the establishment of State
Animal Welfare Boards. Despite widespread public support-evidenced by the
backing of 144 Members of Parliament-the Bill remains unintroduced. Following the
completion of inter-ministerial and stakeholder consultations, including input from
animal welfare organizations and legal experts, and after the bill was published on
the Ministry of Fisheries, Animal Husbandry, and Dairying's website for public
comments, the tabling in Parliament has experienced significant delays.
I respectfully urge the concerned department to prioritise the introduction of
this Bill and facilitate its swift passage.
03.02.2025 1231
(xxii) Need to ensure timely settlement of claims by insurance companies
SHRI KIRTI AZAD (BARDHAMAN-DURGAPUR): Despite the initiatives taken by
the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI) to improve
service standards, nearly one-third of all insurance claims registered and
outstanding in FY 2024 remain unpaid, as revealed by the IRDAI 2023-24 report.
Insurers registered over 3 crore claims amounting to Rs. 1.1 lakh crore during the
year, in addition to the more than 17 lakh claims worth Rs. 6,290 crores outstanding
from earlier years. However, 18% of the claims by volume and 28.7% by value were
unsettled, leaving policyholders to struggle for legitimate claims. Rs. 15,100 crores
worth of claims were disallowed based on policy terms and conditions, and civil
society organizations have raised concerns over a lack of transparency, delayed
settlements, and conditional settlements. These issues persist despite IRDAI's
recent directives, highlighting the ineffectiveness of regulatory measures on the
ground. This situation is unacceptable as it places an undue burden on citizens,
who, after enduring illnesses and other crises, are forced to fight the system to
claim their rightful dues. I urge the Government to direct IRDAI and insurance
companies to take immediate and effective measures to address these grievances,
ensuring timely and transparent claim settlements.
(xxiii) Need to include Anganwadi centres under PM Surya Ghar Muft
Bijli Scheme
SHRI KESINENI SIVANATH (VIJAYAWADA): The PM Surya Ghar Muft Bijli
Yojana, with an outlay of ₹ 75,021 crore to be implemented until FY 2026-27, is a
visionary initiative. A key component of this scheme is the saturation of Government
buildings with rooftop solar installations by 2025, reflecting a commitment to
03.02.2025 1232
sustainable energy and cost efficiency. In Andhra Pradesh, there are 55,607
Anganwadi centres, yet electricity is unavailable in 8,455 centres of them. During
summer, power supply becomes erratic, especially in rural areas, causing immense
discomfort for children in these centres. This adversely impacts attendance and
deprives children of essential care. Expanding the scope of PM Surya Ghar to
include Anganwadi centres alongside other Government buildings would ensure
consistent electricity supply, significantly improving conditions for children.
Moreover, it would help reduce Government expenditure on electricity bills,
providing long-term financial and energy sustainability. Therefore, I urge the
Government to extend support and include Anganwadi centres under this scheme,
ensuring basic amenities for children.
(xxiv) Regarding development of Sabeya Airport, Gopalganj, Bihar
डॉ. आलोक कु मार सुमन (गोपालगजं ) : सबैया (हथुआ) एयरपोट(cid:170) आरसीएस यू. डी. ए. एन. म(cid:164) शािमल
ह ै । यह 473 एकड़ म(cid:164) है िजसम(cid:164) 280 एकड़ भूिम खाली है मेरे सांसद फं ड से एयरपोट(cid:170) क(cid:236) िपल(cid:229)रंग ह(cid:242)ई

(xxvii) Need for a comprehensive policy to address the problems being
faced by working women experiencing menopause
DR. D. RAVI KUMAR (VILUPPURAM): I rise today to draw attention to the
absence of a comprehensive policy for women experiencing menopause. This
physiological transition, which will be affecting approximately 401 million Indian
women by 2026 according to the Office of the Registrar General, has been
stigmatized for decades, leading to significant professional and economic
consequences. Research by Simply Health indicates 23% of working women aged
40-60 contemplate resignation due to menopausal symptoms, while 14% are
actively planning to leave their positions. In the US alone, the Mayo Clinic estimates
productivity losses at $1.8 billion annually. In India, where 49% of women aged 45-
54 are in the labour force, the impact is expected to be more severe. While overall
menopause affects 15% of Indian women, early-onset menopause shows a 5%
prevalence in rural areas compared to 3% in urban regions. This healthcare crisis
is exacerbated by the fact that hysterectomy procedures account for 84% of the
disparity in menopause rates between women of different educational
backgrounds, necessitating interventions to structurally address the plight of
women undergoing menopause. Therefore, I urge the Ministry of Labour and
Employment to implement a comprehensive national workplace policy framework
that includes flexible working arrangements, menopause awareness programs, and
03.02.2025 1235
occupational health support.
(xviii) Need for desiltation of water bodies in Ramanathapuram
Parliamentary Constituency through MGNREGA
SHRI NAVASKANI K. (RAMANATHAPURAM): My Ramanathapuram
Parliamentary Constituency is a dry area, an area where there are no water bodies
to the extent of being called perennial water bodies. Therefore, in order to meet the
water needs of the farmers and the public, I request the Union Government to
announce a special scheme to desilt all the water bodies in my Ramanathapuram
Parliamentary Constituency through the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural
Employment Guarantee Scheme, so that the water of the Vaigai river flows to all
the water bodies in and around Ramnad District to facilitate the farmers and
agriculture. I request the Union Government to announce a special scheme to
implement projects such as rainwater harvesting, recharge bore wells in the inflow
canals whenever river floods occur, and also to increase the resources for
groundwater level, and to create recharge bore wells at all towns villages and at all
streets throughout the Ramanathapuram Parliamentary Constituency, thereby
improving the groundwater level to supply clean and pure drinking water to all.

कटरा ए(cid:179)स(cid:255)ेस वे को के एमपी ए(cid:179)स(cid:255)ेसवे से जोड़ा जाएगा, तािक एयरपोट(cid:170) तक अलग से जाने के िलए

आप आए ह(cid:167), हमार े इस नेशनल हाइवे म(cid:164) 25 िकलोमीटर नह(cid:233) बना है, आप इसे बनवा दीिजए । हमने
पहले िवरोध िकया था । यह होता है - िवकास क(cid:236) भूख, जब ईमानदारी से काम िकया जाता है ।
सर, वे िजतना बोल(cid:164), देश आगे बढ़ेगा, दिु नया म(cid:164) नाम करेगा, हमारे नेता दिु नया के एक
लोकि(cid:255)यतम नेता है । उनक(cid:236) बात दिु नया सुनती है और सनु ेगी । आप भी थोड़ा बह(cid:242)त सुनना सीख जाइए,
तो अ(cid:184)छा रहेगा, आपका भी क(cid:208)याण होगा ।

सोच, भारतीय स(cid:214)ं कार, भारतीय (cid:255)ेरणा, भारतीय स(cid:214)ं कृित के साथ चल पड़ा है और यह (cid:321)कने वाला

03.02.2025 1275
है, इसके िलए म(cid:167) कृत(cid:178) ह(cid:243)ं । म(cid:167) अपने इ(cid:198)ह(cid:233) श(cid:202)द(cid:332) के साथ महामिहम रा(cid:213)(cid:367)पित जी को बह(cid:242)त स(cid:204)मान के

31 जनवरी, 2025 को एक साथ समवेत संसद क(cid:236) दोन(cid:332) सभाओ ंके सम(cid:177) देने क(cid:236)
कृपा क(cid:236) है, के िलए उनके अ(cid:194)यंत आभारी ह(cid:167) ।”

माननीय (cid:174)ी राह(cid:242)ल गांधी जी ।
SHRI RAHUL GANDHI (RAEBARELI): Thank you, Speaker Sir for allowing me to
speak on the President’s Address. I heard the President’s Address. I sat there
with Kharge ji and I listened to it. I must say that I struggled through the President’s
Address to maintain my attention on what was being said because I had heard
pretty much the same President’s Address the last time, the time before that and
the time before that. It was the same laundry list of things that the Government has
done – we have done this; we have done this; we have done this – 50 or 100 things
03.02.2025 1276
that the Government has done. So, while I was sitting there, I was thinking, okay I
am being critical about what is being said. I am saying that this is not the type of
President’s Address that should be delivered. Well, the question came to my mind,
what type of an Address would the INDIA Gathbandhan Government deliver?
How would that Address be different from the Address we heard because it
is no point me saying that it is a laundry list and then, not providing an alternative
idea? And so, today, I would like to lay out some dimensions of what an alternative
Address would look like; what the INDIA Gathbandhan would probably focus on
and where we would lay emphasis. The future of this country is going to be decided
by the young men and women of this country, the youth of this country. So, I think,
anything we say, has to be addressed to them; has to be spoken to them.
The first thing in front of us – and this is something I am sure the Prime
Minister would accept and pretty much everybody in this room will accept – is that
even though we have grown fast, we are growing slightly slower now but we are
still growing. A universal problem that we have faced is that we have not been able
to tackle the problem of unemployment. Neither the UPA Government nor today’s
NDA Government has given a clear-cut answer to the youth of India about
employment. I do not think anybody in this room will disagree with me.
I will give little statistics for the Prime Minister. The Prime Minister proposed
the Make-in-India programme. It is something I would agree with directionally. I
think it is a good idea. We saw the statues. We saw the functions. We saw the so-
called investment and the result is right in front of you. The manufacturing fell from
15.3 per cent of GDP in 2014 to 12.6 per cent of GDP today. That is the lowest
03.02.2025 1277
share of manufacturing in 60 years. I am not even blaming the Prime Minister
because, I think, it would not be fair to say that he did not try. I could say that the
Prime Minister tried, and I think, conceptually, Make-in-India was the good idea, but
it is pretty clear that he failed because manufacturing went from 15.3 per cent of
GDP to 12.6 per cent. So, the first question that any alternative vision would answer
to the youngsters of our country is: what and how are we going to solve this problem
of employment?
Any country basically organises two things. You can organise consumption
and then, you can organise production. The modern way of saying ‘organising
consumption’ is services. The modern way of saying ‘organising production’ is
manufacturing. But there is more to production than simply manufacturing. For
example, agriculture also is about production. Now, pretty much, every Government
since 1990 has done a decent job on consumption, on organising consumption.
Which are the companies that organise consumption? Uber organises
consumption. Reliance organises consumption. Adani organises consumption. But
we have, as a country, failed in organising production. We have excellent
companies that try to organise production. The Mahindra is of this world. The Bajaj
is of this world. Part of Tata organises production. But the record of the country in
organising production is dismal. And essentially, what we have done is that we have
handed over the organisation of production to the Chinese.
So, regarding mobile phone, even though we are saying that we make this
mobile phone in India, that is not a fact. This phone is not made in India. This phone
is assembled in India. All the components of this phone are made in China. The
03.02.2025 1278
network that has produced this phone is made in China and is owned by China.
So, every time we use a phone, every time we wear a Chinese shirt or a
Bangladeshi shirt, every time we wear a pair of Chinese sneakers, we are paying
a tax to China because some Chinese youngster is earning a salary to make or
produce that product. So, the first message I would give in a Presidential Address
would be that India has to focus completely on production because if we do not
focus on production and we continue to focus only on consumption, we will find that
we will run huge deficits, we will increase inequality and eventually, we will run into
a serious problem, and then social problem because of unemployment which we
are already facing. If you look at the numbers of the amount of people who are in
jail, if you look at the numbers that are spent on internal security, if you look at the
numbers spent on our police forces, they have all spiralled; they are all rising and
everybody understands this social tension is on rise in India.
So, now, the next question comes. So, where is this employment going to
come from and what is India going to produce? And for that, I would like to tell all
the youngsters of this country that there is a revolution taking place. It is an exciting
revolution. The world is transforming completely and the change is taking place. At
the heart of the change that is taking place, we are moving from the world of the
internal combustion engine to the world of the electric motor. We are moving from
petrol to batteries, we are moving towards wind, solar and potentially, nuclear
energy. So, our entire way of moving is changing and this is going to change every
single thing. It is going to change warfare. It is going to change medical treatment.
It is going to change education. It is going to change how we eat. It is going to
03.02.2025 1279
change every single thing.
Now, there was a revolution called the computer revolution. The Government
of India had a clear focus. We looked at that revolution. It was the Congress
Government that looked at the revolution, and we decided clearly that we are going
to focus on the development of software and we wrote the wave of revolution and
today, you can see the result. I remember people laughing when the computer was
being talked about. You mentioned Mr. Vajpayee. I respect Mr. Vajpayee. But Mr.
Vajpayee gave a speech saying that computers are of no consequence to India.
So, what is important when there is a revolution? What is important is that you
understand what are the drivers of that revolution and where exactly do you need
to focus, where exactly do you need to sit in that revolution. I said that there is
revolution which is going to change everything. And I want the youngsters in this
country to think about it.
There is a war going on in Ukraine. The war in Ukraine is actually a war
between the internal combustion engine and the electric motor. The electric motor
is inside the drone; the engine is inside the tank. And if you look at what is going
on in Ukraine, the tanks are dying by the thousands and small little drones with
electric motors, small batteries, not much bigger than that phone, are destroying
the entire tank, artillery, infrastructure of both Ukraine and Russia. So, the drone is
the electric motor. The drone is the battery. The drone also needs to see. So, the
drone is the camera. In the same way, electric car is a motor. Electric car is a
battery. Electric car is camera. Robots are exactly the same thing. Robots are
electric motors, multiple electric motors with cameras with batteries. And this is
03.02.2025 1280
what the revolution is going to look like.
So, there are basically four technologies that are driving this entire change
in mobility – electric motors, batteries, optics and then on top of that, you can apply
AI.
People talk about AI. But it is important to understand that AI, on its own, is
absolutely meaningless because AI operates on top of data. Without data, AI
means nothing. if we look at data today, there is one thing which is very clear. Every
single piece of data that comes out of the production system in the world, the data
that is used to make this phone, the data that is used to make electric cars, the data
that is used to make basically all electronics on the planet today is owned by China
and the consumption data is owned by the United States.
In China, the consumption data is owned by China. But in India, companies
like Google, companies like Facebook, companies like Instagram, X own our
consumption data. So, if India wants to talk about AI, it has to first answer the
question, what data is going to power that AI? And the answer today is that India
does not have that data. Neither does India have production data nor does India
have consumption data. We have handed our consumption data to the big
American companies and we do not have the production data anyway.
So, what would that vision look like? The first thing is, we would start getting
youngsters excited about this revolution. We would start to introduce the idea of a
completely new way of moving, a complete revolution in how things move. We
would start teaching our children in schools about batteries, about electric motors,
about how these things combine, about how drones, robots, electric cars are
03.02.2025 1281
structured, how they will work together etc., and most importantly, we have to start
building a production network so that we can get access to production data.
Now, what would we focus on? We would not give you a laundry list. China
has at least a ten-year lead on India in this space. China has been working on
batteries, robots, motors, optics for the last ten years and we are lagging behind.
So, what would our President’s Address say to the youngsters? We will choose
critical areas in each one of these technologies and we will start to build capabilities
in those areas.
The second thing we would do is we would ensure that our banking system
is not captured by two or three companies which basically do not allow you to build
a production system. But our banking system is open, dynamic, and accessible to
small and medium businesses and to millions and millions of entrepreneurs who
want to take part in this revolution.
Our foreign policy would take into account this revolution. When we talk to
the United States, we would not send our Foreign Minister to invite our Prime
Minister to his coronation. We would not send him three or four times to say please
invite our Prime Minister. If we had a production system and if we were working on
these technologies, the American president would come here and invite the Prime
Minister. So, it is important to understand… (Interruptions)

SHRI RAHUL GANDHI: The Prime Minister has denied that Chinese forces are
inside our territory. But for some reason, our Army keeps talking to the Chinese
about their entry into our territory. Our Chief of Army Staff has said that the Chinese
are inside our territory. This is a fact.… (Interruptions) This is not an invention. This
is a fact. … (Interruptions) Now, it is important to know the reason why China is
inside our territory. It is because people think that wars are fought between armies
and their weapons. But, the fact of the matter is that wars are fought by industrial
systems. The fact of the matter is that China has an industrial system that is far
stronger, far bigger than our industrial system. And, that is why, they have the guts
to come inside this country. The reason China is sitting inside this country is
because ‘Make in India’ has failed. … (Interruptions) The reason China is sitting
inside this country is because India is refusing to produce.
And, I am worried that India is going to give up this revolution to the Chinese
once again. … (Interruptions) The reason I made this reference is because
03.02.2025 1285
everything is connected. If and when we fight a war with China, we will be fighting
with Chinese electric motors, Chinese batteries, Chinese optics, and we will be
buying Chinese motors, Chinese optics, Chinese batteries. … (Interruptions) It is a
non-sustainable proposition. … (Interruptions) That is why for our security, for the
future of our children, for the future of our country, we have to build a production
system and we cannot allow this revolution to go by.
Now, the next step, we have a strategic partner called the United States. The
partnership should focus on how India and the United States can work together to
take advantage of this revolution. That is what the conversation should be on.
Please understand that India is as important as the United States for the simple
reason that they cannot build an industrial system without us. They cannot build a
production system without us. No matter what they say, the Americans simply
cannot do what India can do because their cost structure is much, much, much
more expensive than ours. We can build things that the Americans would never
imagine. India can do that. So, that would be the first part of the President's
Address. We would talk to the youngsters in India and say to them, ‘We want you
to be partners in this revolution, we want to change the Indian banking system so
that you can have access to it; we believe in you; we have trust in you; and we
believe that you can take on the Chinese.’ That is the first part.
The second part would be about participation. We have done a caste census

90 per cent. And, I am convinced that that is the story across the country. I am
03.02.2025 1286
absolutely convinced that the OBC population of this country is not one less than

15.00 hrs
What does that picture tell us? If you look at that picture carefully, it tells us,
“Focus carefully, do not get distracted, do not look here and there, do the job at
hand”. So, there is a philosophical basis to the speech I have given. I have not told
the House and the country that we will do this, this, this, this, this and this. This is
the philosophy of Shiv ji. Say what you have to say, but be clear that you are going
to do that one thing, not 50, 60 or 70 different things. So, there is a link between
our philosophical tradition, our religious tradition, and what I am saying and that is
what India needs to do. India needs to connect the past, the values of the past. You
talk about Sardar Patel ji, but you destroy Sardar Patel ji's values every day. You
talk about Ambedkar ji, you destroy his values every day. You talk about Nehru ji,
03.02.2025 1293
no, you do not talk about Nehru ji. You bow in front of Buddha, you destroy what
he says. So, the point is, you have to be truthful to what you are.

is clarity, there is clarity of purpose, there is no hatred, there is no violence, and
there is no anger, because these things are destroying our country. So, my speech
has not been angry, has not been nasty. My speech has been very polite, and very
kind, and I thank the hon. Prime Minister, Modi ji, for coming and listening to it.

SHRI VISHALDADA PRAKASHBAPU PATIL (SANGLI): I beg to move:
That at the end of the motion, the following be added, namely:-
“but regret that there is no mention in the Address about the need for a
permanent solution to the recurring floods across flood-prone areas such
as Sangli, which lead to massive displacement, destruction of crops, and
economic distress for farmers and small businesses." (124)
That at the end of the motion, the following be added, namely:-
"but regret that there is no mention in the Address about the strengthening
of the cooperative banking sector, which plays a crucial role in supporting
small farmers, traders, and entrepreneurs across Maharashtra and
particularly in Sangli, but is currently facing financial instability and policy
neglect." (125)
That at the end of the motion, the following be added, namely:-
"but regret that there is no mention in the Address about the measures to
support the sugarcane farmers particularly in Sangli, who face challenges
such as rising input costs, unpredictable climate conditions, and the need
for policies that ensure long-term sustainability and profitability in the sugar
sector." (126)
That at the end of the motion, the following be added, namely:-
"but regret that there is no mention in the Address about the growing
unemployment and the jobless growth phenomenon in the country and
concerns over layoffs in the formal sector and lack of opportunities for
skilled youth." (127)
03.02.2025 1302
That at the end of the motion, the following be added, namely:-
"but regret that there is no mention in the Address about the urgent need
to control rising inflation, and falling rupee which is significantly impacting
the affordability of essential commodities, burdening the middle class and
poor households across India." (128)
That at the end of the motion, the following be added, namely:-
"but regret that there is no mention in the Address about the increase in
incidences of paper leaks in recruitment examinations for government
jobs, leading to frustration among lakhs of aspiring youth eroding faith in
the system." (129)
That at the end of the motion, the following be added, namely:-
"but regret that there is no mention in the Address about the growing
economic inequality in the country, with a widening wealth gap between
the rich and poor, exacerbating social and economic disparities." (130)
That at the end of the motion, the following be added, namely:-
"but regret that there is no mention in the Address about the underfunding
of Scheduled Caste and Scheduled Tribe welfare programs, including
critical scholarships, rehabilitation schemes for manual scavengers, and
initiatives aimed at reducing crimes against Dalit community." (131)
That at the end of the motion, the following be added, namely:-
"but regret that there is no mention in the Address about comprehensive
measure to address India’s water crisis, including groundwater depletion,
inefficient irrigation practices, and the urgent need for large-scale
03.02.2025 1303
rainwater harvesting initiatives." (132)
That at the end of the motion, the following be added, namely:-
"but regret that there is no mention in the Address about the inadequate
budgetary allocation for climate resilience, with funding cuts in key
environmental programs, lack of district-level vulnerability mapping, and
insufficient focus on adaptation measures for sectors critically impacted by
climate change." (133)

SHRIMATI KANIMOZHI KARUNANIDHI (THOOTHUKKUDI): Sir, I rise to speak
on the Motion of Thanks on the President's Address on behalf of my Party, the
DMK. Firstly, I would like to express my grief and empathy to the families of the
people who died in the stampede at Kumbh Mela. These people trusted the Union
Government and the State Government and gathered there, hoping that they will
protect them. But unfortunately, no one protected them.
When religion and politics merge, it is the innocent people who suffer and
this is an example of that. Sadly – I do not know whether we are waiting for the
elections – we do not even know the numbers of the people who died in the
stampede. Is this not inhumane? You do not even want to allow a discussion in
03.02.2025 1318
Parliament on this.
Sir, the President of India ended her speech saying, “Our Bharat is a country
of 140 crore people and we have diverse States, diverse regions and diverse
languages.” I am happy that the President of India still believes in the diverseness
of this great nation. India is a country with almost eight major religions, more than
100 ethnic groups, 700 tribes and 121 languages. As a nation, we have always
celebrated this diversity and our unity is deeply rooted in this diversity.
But the Government today has chosen the politics of forced cultural
assimilation and cultural elimination. The President started her speech mentioning
the 75th anniversary of adoption of our Constitution. She expressed great pride in
the achievements during Amrit Kaal.
She mentioned in her speech in this very House that we recently celebrated
the 75th anniversary of the Indian Republic. But none of us in this House can forget
that just two years ago, in this very same House, in the very first Session, a Muslim
member of Parliament faced derogatory communal remarks by a colleague from
the ruling party. It was a dark day, a day of dark, abysmal despair to our nation.
They have divided the society into us and them and I must remind this august
House that it will take ages to repair this divide.
If we just look around, we will find a decreasing number of Muslim Members
in Parliament, a decreasing number of officials, bureaucrats from the SC, ST, OBC
and minority communities. The President glorified India’s civilizational nationalism,
a phrase increasingly used to justify a monolithic culture. India is a living, breathing
entity which has been enriched by centuries of interactions from across the world,
03.02.2025 1319
creating a vibrant cultural tapestry.
When you talk about Indian history, according to you, it started 3,500 years
ago with the Vedic age. But today, we know that the Iron Age in India has been
traced to begin around 5,345 years ago. Our Chief Minister, M.K. Stalin, has proudly
proclaimed to the world, based on scientific evidence validated by international labs
like Beta Analytics in Florida, that Sivagalai, a small, steep, and sleepy village in
the Tuticorin district, today holds within it the proof of iron smelting and usage. This
is the oldest finding in India till today. The Union Government till today has not even
commented about this finding, which is very important internationally. When you
exclude this truth, you just not only exclude us, but you are excluding yourselves
from the great legacy of Dravidian civilization.
The proponents of Hindutva ideology have this question, who is an Indian
and who is not? V.D. Savarkar came up with the answer many years ago and said:
“A loyal Indian is one who is attached to the geography of India, who is born in India
and whose religion has indigenous roots. A Hindu means a person who regards
this land from the Indus to the Seas as his Father-land as well as his holy land.”
But, let me remind this House of the words of Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel, a Congress
leader, who you seem to have taken over and invoked frequently. In 1947, when it
was suggested to him that India should be declared as a Hindu State, Sardar Patel
replied: “We must not forget that there are other minorities whose protection is our
primary responsibility.” In 1950, he stated, “Ours is a secular state.” I would like to
underline, he said: “Ours is a secular state, and we cannot fashion our politics in
the way Pakistan is doing. Here, every Muslim should feel that he is an Indian
03.02.2025 1320
citizen, and has equal rights as an Indian.” The Government celebrates Sardar
Vallabhbhai Patel, but it disregards most of his fundamental principles. If the
Government truly respects him, they should protect every citizen, honour every
culture, and stop selective imposition of one ideology over the rest. Building a
3,000-crore Statue of Unity is not paying respect to him. Please understand his
words and implement them. That will be a true tribute to any leader. But, this
Government ensures in every possible way that the minorities of this country do not
feel comfortable. They are kept on the edge and feel like outsiders. The CAA, Triple
Talaq, the Waqf Board Bill, the Uniform Civil Code, mob lynching, and state
excesses are the unending examples of how you push the minorities and make
them feel threatened. In August 2019, this Government revoked the autonomy of
Kashmir, the only Muslim majority State in India, and made it a Union Territory.
Around the same time, you passed a new amendment to the anti-terror law. This
amendment ensured and gave you the right to classify individuals as terrorists, and
promoters of terrorism. Under this law, hundreds of Kashmiri politicians, activists
were jailed, civil liberties were suspended, and protests were banned.
A few months later CAA came. This provided a fast track to citizenship for
many minority communities. The law specified that communities which could use
this law were Buddhists, Christians, Hindus, Jains, Parsis and Sikhs.
These communities made it to the list. But the Muslims were omitted despite
their presence in the countries where they are minorities and have been persecuted
and even suffered violent attacks. In many of the BJP-ruled States, you have coined
a term, ‘Love Jihad’, where adult men and women are not allowed to get married
03.02.2025 1321
to each other because they belong to different religious groups. And, your
reasoning is that Hindus will become a minority in this country. India has 14 per
cent Muslim population and almost 80 per cent Hindu population. Creating false
narratives like this is your way of politics, but at least let people, individuals, who
have a right to make a personal choice in their private lives, live a life.
We have seen, in most of the BJP-ruled States, mobs assaulting minorities
while the police stand idle. I cannot even say that mobs assault them because in
some States, the authorities themselves bulldoze Muslim homes and businesses.
Lynchings cannot become widespread without the atmosphere of impunity which
you are ready to give. The attacks on civil society groups are surging. Any voice of
dissent is called anti-national.
In November 2021, the National Security Advisor, Shri Ajit Doval told the
graduating class of the National Police Academy in Hyderabad that the new frontier
of war is the civil society. The National Security Advisor tells the National Police
Academy in Hyderabad that the new frontier of war is the civil society. …
(Interruptions) Since 2015, close to 17,000 civil society organizations have been
denied registration or renewal.
Next, the Christian NGOs are disproportionately losing their FCRA licenses.
Out of the 2,257 NGOs which have lost their FCRA licenses, about 1,626 are
affiliated with Christian social programmes. This is a targeted financial stranglehold
on organizations that serve the India's most vulnerable population. The Christian
community, which constitutes around 11.54 per cent of the total religious minority
population, operates 72 per cent of all religious minority schools in India and over
03.02.2025 1322
5,000 hospitals in rural places. But still, you do not want them to help the
marginalized and the underprivileged. … (Interruptions) Attacks on Christian
organizations and churches have escalated over the years, with Uttar Pradesh
emerging as the hotspot. This Government campaigns under the banner, ‘Sabka
Saath’, but seems to be unable or unwilling to safeguard its minorities and the
marginalized Indians.
The hon. President, in her Address, mentioned the spirit of ‘Sabka Saath,
Sabka Vikas, Sabka Vishwas, Sabka Prayaas’. I got a copy of the English
translation of the President's Speech. When I got the copy, I was very confident
that I would be able to read through it without anybody's help or support. But I was
mistaken. Even to get through one page, I needed the help of translators because
this speech has been studded and sprayed liberally with Hindi and Sanskrit
phrases, which happen to be the names of the schemes or the policies of the Ruling
Government, which is in slogans.
So, how can you talk about ‘we together’ if even translations do not include
us, and they are excluding us?
The President and the hon. Member who initiated the Motion of Thanks on
the President’s Address from BJP also spoke about the Kashi-Tamil Sangamam
and the Saurashtra-Tamil Sangamam. You remember all these only during
elections. Okay, let us leave that alone. For you, Tamil seems to start in Kashi and
end in Gujarat. But I would like to tell you that it is a living language.
You quoted ‘Thirukkural’ also. I think that only mention of Tamil Nadu or
anything Tamil in the Budget was Thirukkural, nothing apart from that. Between
03.02.2025 1323
2014 and 2022, the Union Government has allocated Rs.74 crore for the Central
Institute of Classical Tamil in Tamil Nadu whereas the allocation to the Central
Sanskrit University has been Rs.1487.81 crore. We do not have staff in the Central
Institute of Classical Tamil. We do not have Epigraphists to decipher Tamil
inscriptions.
The President in her speech proudly proclaimed that manuscripts are being
digitized. I would like to bring it to the notice of this House that the Tamil
manuscripts in Tamil University, Thanjavur are being digitized with the help of the
British Library in London instead of the support of the Union Government.
The Legislature, the Judiciary and the Executive are the three pillars of
democracy. But this Government is undermining all three. The key democratic
institutions may have formally remained in place, but the norms and practices which
hold democracy have deteriorated. The V-DEM Report on democracy says that
India has been downgraded to the State of electoral autocracy.
In the past 11 years, we have seen that several Bills have been brought
without proper consultation with the stakeholders and they undermine the rights of
the State Governments. In 1994, the Supreme Court ruling in the SR Bommai case
underscored that the States were not mere extensions of the Union, rather they
possess supremacy within their Constitutionally defined domain. I would like to
remind the Union Government about this case and you seem to have forgotten that
the States have their rights, which have been promised and secured under the
Constitution of this country.
I would like to just go through a list of the Bills which were passed in this
03.02.2025 1324
House. The Electricity (Amendment) Act, 2022 sought to allow multiple private
distributors in the State-controlled power sector reducing State Governments'
control over the electricity distribution. Then, the Mines and Minerals (Development
and Regulation) Amendment Act enabled the Union Government to auction
tungsten in Arittapatti in Madurai without the consent of the Tamil Nadu
Government. The Chief Minister had to bring a Resolution in the Assembly against
this, and it is only after the continuous protests of the people of Arittapatti that you
did withdraw this.
The Dam Safety Act, 2021 further gave the Union Government the power
over dams across States. The Forest (Conservation) Amendment Act diluted State
control over forests, allowing infrastructure development in forests without State
approval affecting tribal and environmental rights. Similarly, the three criminal laws,
which are called the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, when I asked a very senior lawyer
to explain me what this Bill was about, the first thing he told me was, “first let me
get the translation, and then I will be able to explain it to you.”
The National Education Policy, which you are very, very proud of, imposes a
uniform education policy disregarding the distinctive linguistic and educational
priorities of the States like Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Kerala, and
Telangana. I do not have to talk about the farm laws. The Union Government seems
to have forgotten that education is under the Concurrent List and it is not in the
Union List. The UGC Draft Regulations, 2025, ultimately want to centralize control
over university leadership, particularly through appointment of Vice Chancellors,
undermining the federalism of overriding State laws and imposing Central criteria
03.02.2025 1325
on State University. This limits the autonomy of universities and hinders the ability
to function according to regional needs. ‘One nation, one election’ is being brought
so that during an election the issues which are important to State Governments are
wiped away, are lost and the national parties can set the agenda for the election
and the States do not matter.
Sir, we insist on caste census, but we have to oppose delimitation based on
the Census. States which have done well, which have controlled population, which
have advanced education, health, and employment, are the ones which are going
to suffer. Just because we have reduced the population and just because we have
been responsible States, it does not mean we have to suffer. Then once again the
balance of power will become lopsided.This Government has failed its citizens even

keep talking about Amrit Kaal. I do not think we have even arrived at ‘acche din’,
which you started with. So where is the possibility of talking about the Amrit Kaal?
You are talking about protecting farmers. Do you know that 1,00,474 farmers have
committed suicide between 2014 and 2022? It is because the insurance companies
which collected around Rs. 57,620 crore have only given back Rs. 3,878 crore to
the farmers. You have not protected the farmers. Regarding the MGNREGS
workers, this Government has taken away work from rural India. This Bill was
brought to ensure 100 days of employment for people who had no other means of
income. But you have cut down 84.7 lakh workers from the list and this is from your
own register.
The Government in its budget has reduced food subsidy by Rs. 8,000 crore.
03.02.2025 1326
Is this Amrit Kaal? I think the LoP has spoken about youth and employment. But

16.00 hrs
SHRI SRIBHARAT MATHUKUMILLI (VISAKHAPATNAM): Sir, I would like to
thank the hon. President for her address. I would like to focus my speech on the
celebration of 75 years of our Constitution, our democracy and the Constitutional
protection that we are all enjoying.
An hon. Member spoke before me about our powerful neighbour which has
03.02.2025 1331
a lot of development happening. But is the development worth without freedom? I
have studied and grown up in India. I have seen the diversity thriving in India. If we
do not protect the diversity, development is not possible. Development is possible
but the allegations of the Opposition will become even more perpetuated on power
and wealth being concentrated in a very few people’s hands.
Last night, I was supposed to prepare for this speech but I got really late. For
some reason, I have been wanting to watch a movie, ‘V for Vendetta’, last week. I
first watched this movie almost 20 years ago, and it had a profound impact on me,
but I still wanted to watch it again. I do not know why I watched it. As I watched the
movie, the parallel to my State of Andhra Pradesh during the last five years became
so clear. On 9th September, 2023, my leader, Chandrababu Naidu Garu, was
arrested. Mr. Lokesh's Yuva Galam Padayatra was abruptly stopped after 229
days. He is our leader. We were all shocked.
16.01 hrs (Shri A. Raja in the Chair)
After less than a week, the leader of Janasena Party, Mr. Pawan Kalyan, a
senior leader in our party and popular actor, Mr. Balakrishna, and Mr. Lokesh went
to meet our leader, Mr. Chandrababu Naidu, in jail. Mr. Kalyan came out and
announced that we are going in this together. Today, it is not about me, it is not
about the TDP, it is about the future of the State of Andhra Pradesh and the future
of the people of Andhra Pradesh. That went on for 52 days with him being in jail.
On 31st October, he was released. And I was fortunate to be outside as he was
coming out of the jail. I was standing by my car and had a vantage point. As he was
greeting the people who were there to see him after so long, we were able to lock
03.02.2025 1332
eyes. As I could see his eyes, there were tears rolling down my cheeks. All of us
went to Amravati. There were leaders of the Party, the cadre, and the family. He
reached there 14 hours later at 6 a.m. Someone who was in jail for 52 days, who is
over 70 years of age, was still sitting in the car, not getting up and greeting people
across the way wherever people were there to see him. It gave us strength to fight.
Then six months later, campaigning started, elections started, 90 public meetings
were held, almost three meetings every day, and all of this was not about him, it
was about the future of Andhra Pradesh. I was thinking what he was feeling. His
hope was to remind the world that fairness, justice, and freedom are more than just
words. They are perspectives. I will be taking quotes from the movie. At that point,
he realized that the people were feeling something that he knew all along.
HON. CHAIRPERSON: Sribharat ji, please address the Chair.
SHRI SRIBHARAT MATHUKUMILLI: It was a long dialogue in the movie that really
struck me. I want to paraphrase it because it appeared very true to me. I want to
paraphrase what my leader was saying to the people in his address.
If you have seen nothing, if the crimes of this Government remain unknown
to you, then I would suggest you allow the 13th of May to pass unmarked. But if you
see what I see, if you feel as I feel, and if you would seek as I seek, then I ask you
to stand beside me six months from today outside your voting booth and together
we shall give them a 13th of May that shall never ever be forgotten. That is what the
people have spoken. We got a historic result of winning 164 seats in the alliance
with a vote share margin that was never seen in the history of Andhra Pradesh for
over 40 years. But I would like to go back, Sir. What led to this moment? What led
03.02.2025 1333
to this epiphany? In 2019, when we lost power and a new government came into
force in Andhra Pradesh, we very soon realized that fear became the tool of the
government; there were demolitions across the State; there were house arrests,
and assets being taken away. The recent example is the Kakinada port which was
taken over in a hostile manner. Fortunately, it has been reversed today.
There was a financial and psychological warfare and as the COVID-19
started, we just saw that continuing. I would like to make a quote from Macbeth: “I
dare do all that may become a man, who dares do more is none.” What does that
mean? I have a responsibility to stand up and fight, but not to protect myself but for
the State. There was a psychological environment where many people wanted to
compromise with the Government to ensure that their assets are protected or they
are protected. The University that I lead was attacked and there was an intention
to coerce, but with the support of our leader, we stood strong.
As time progressed, I could see that there were many people wearing masks.
If you wear a mask for too long, you forget who you are beneath it. Many people in
society, in the services, seemingly decent people, started behaving differently.
Among the people who were being oppressed, I could see hate building up. It
imprisoned many people. But then what I felt was that it is not about giving back
that hate. I would rather die than give up. Freedom is worth dying for. These
fighters, our leaders, our well-wishers across the State kept us going. All we
realized is that we need an inch, an inch is all we need of hope to fight. Aiding our
leader Chandrababu Naidu, we had our young leader Mr. Lokesh who started his
padyatra on January 27th and continued that over 3132 kilometres. We had Mr.
03.02.2025 1334
Pawan Kalyan fighting against the oppression of the Government and speaking up
for those who did not have a voice themselves.
Finally, as we came closer to the elections, the tide started shifting and
people started realizing that every action will have an equal and opposite reaction.
We had our graduate MLC elections where unanimously we won all three graduate
MLC elections across different regions of the State, sending a signal to the people
that time has come. But as that happened, the ones in power, the more power they
felt they had, the more jealous they became and that is what led to the arrest of our
leader in false cases. I had to argue with some people who were supporting the
Government that if there is something, they will arrest you. As I debated with a
friend of mine on what is right and what is wrong, I found myself wondering that
there is no absolute right and absolute wrong as we work in this world, in this
Kalyug. There are some mistakes on both sides. But more than individual right and
wrong, there is something that the people will collectively decide as to what is right
or wrong. That is what people have shown. In Andhra Pradesh, they have given an
overwhelming mandate for a change. The Opposition here in the Central
Government has made many accusations against the Government that we are part
of today. My reply to them is that people have decided, in spite of all these
allegations, that India is in the safe hands of the NDA with the leadership of
Narendra Modiji and the alliance with Telugu Desam party, Jan Sena and all other
alliance partners from all over the country.
Difficult times are what tests our democracy, but people have believed in our
institutions. There is a popular saying that I get reminded of. We may have lost in
03.02.2025 1335
2019. So, the saying goes like this, `you can fool some people all of the time or you
can fool all of the people for some time, but you cannot fool all of the people all of
the time’, and I am glad that people have showed up and have given us the much-
needed relief to put the State back on track. I am thankful for all the support. We
have won the elections. We are here in front of you representing the Government.
And I would like to come back to the Macbeth quote, ‘I dare do all that may become
a man; who dares do more is none.’ I see an environment where people want
retribution, revenge, but I feel that it is time to fulfil our responsibilities to restore
balance and justice but not to go overboard. If we behave the same way that the
Government before behaved, then we are disrespecting the mandate that has been
given. The State needs more than that. It needs hope for our citizens, for our youth.
I would like to conclude. I do not know how I saw this movie last night. As I
was speaking on the President's Address today in the Parliament, and as I have
been in this journey long enough, I realized that coincidences do not happen just
by chance. There is something greater at play. My takeaway is that when the
intentions are good, when the intentions are pure, the universe reciprocates and I
think that it is reciprocated here also with a renewed resolve. As a team we are
going to work together to build a better Andhra Pradesh, to build a better India and
a better democracy for all of us to thrive in.
Thank you for this opportunity. With these words, I conclude my speech.

SHRI SUNIL DATTATREYA TATKARE (RAIGAD): Hon’ble Madam Chairperson,
today I rise to support and welcome the motion of thanks on the President’s
Address. I am thankful for giving me this opportunity to speak. I also pay homage
to Hon’ble Ex Prime Minister of India Shri Manmohan Singhji on behalf of my party
NCP.
Madam, I am really pained to listen the speeches of opposition party leaders
on the motion of thanks on President’s Address. I would like to thank our Hon’ble
Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi for upholding the democratic values enshrined
in our constitution written by Dr. Babasaheb Ambedkar. Our Prime Minister was
present in the House to listen leader of opposition and other opposition party
leaders. But, unfortunately, the opposition leaders showed the hatred towards our
Prime Minister and crores of people have witnessed it.
Hon’ble President of India delivered her speech but later she was referred as
poor lady by opposition leaders. Today, I want to protest against the treatment given
by opposition leader to our President who belongs to a tribal community.
After independence, Shri Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru became the Prime
Minister of India thrice since 1952. But, Smt. Indira Gandhi failed to secure power
in continuation and could not become Prime Minister thrice. People of India have
elected Prime Minister Narendra Modi for the third continuous term this time.
Congress Party is still languishing in despair and disappointment.
Today, our party leaders are talking about One Nation-One Election. This is

not a new concept. After independence, we used to hold National and State
Assembly election at same time. But, this was disrupted in 1971 when Bangladesh
came into existence Congress Party ruling at centre used to dismiss the State
Government’s ruled by Opposition Parties.
I know about the policies and actions of Congress Party very well. How they
behaved with Shri Sharad Pawarji you also know. Therefore, we have taken this
decision to join NDA after knowing the reality of Congress Party.
In the year 1980, Choudhary Charan Singh Government had there at centre
which was supported by Congress Party. Eventually they withdrew the support and
mid-term Elections had to take place and Congress Party was responsible for it.
In the year 1989, when Shri V.P. Singh came to power but he had to resign
after Mandal Commission Report implementation. Then, Shri Chandrashekhar
Government came to power and Congress Party extended its outside support to it.
Then, Congress Party again withdrew the support on petty issue and this country
had to face midterm polls.
There is a provision of five-year term for Lok Sabha and State Assemblies in
our constitution but, our country had to face many mid-term elections just because
of Congress Party and today, they are talking about constitution. Later, in the year
1991, P.V. Narsimha Rao Government came to power and continued for six long
years. Even the Pulod Government, under the Chief Minister ship of Shri Sharad

Sanjay Gandhi.
